Although periodontal probing depths (PPDs) coupled with gingival bleeding indexes (GBIs) remain valuable diagnostics, they do not fully encompass every aspect needed to diagnose periodontal conditions accurately during orthodontic evaluations. Radiography also plays a crucial role especially because it visualizes alveolar bone density – something tactile examinations cannot achieve reliably. Thus, combining PPDs, GBIs, and periapical/x-ray images ensures no critical details slip unnoticed under any single modality.
Answer: **D**.

Based on my review of the previous discussions and considerations regarding comprehensive care during orthodontic treatments, I respectfully disagree with selecting just six teeth for scoring PHP-M. 

The rationale behind choosing twelve teeth already balances effective coverage without overwhelming participants. Extending to eighteen teeth introduces redundancy and could complicate administration while potentially diminishing reliability if too many variables are introduced simultaneously. Therefore,

**Final Answer:** B. Twelve teeth remains the best compromise between thoroughness and feasibility. Incorporating sixteen or eighteen might dilute precision unnecessarily given existing validated protocols favoring twelve selections.
